title: No budget, treasury, or resource check anywhere — money never comes from anywhere
evidence: `PublicFinances.treasury_reserve_usd_m` = 210 (kestral-strait.json:30, macro_schema.py:31-33) and per-agent `budget_control_usd_m` (agent_schema.py:136; e.g. defence 340 at kestral-strait.json:287, head-of-government 0 at :259) are both parsed and never read. `Intervention.resource_cost` (agent_schema.py:239-241) and `ActionProposal` have no cost path into the engine — grep for `resource_cost` in backend/ returns only the schema line. Every entry in `ACTION_EFFECTS` (engine.py:35-43) touches only `military_readiness`, `government_approval` and `social_stability_index`; no economic or fiscal key appears. `apply_deltas` (macro_state.py:23-47) only handles top-level scalars and explicitly skips nested blocks (macro_state.py:26-28), so `public_finances` is unreachable by any delta even if one were authored.
why_it_matters: Directly contradicts CHARTER.md:19 "Money has to come from somewhere" and the charter's "Spend or reserve resources → Resource" primitive (CHARTER.md:94). Procurement acceleration is free, forever. Treasury reserve is a decorative number that can never change, which also means the finance minister's stated objective `preserve_treasury_runway` (kestral-strait.json:301) is unfalsifiable.
remedy: Extend `apply_deltas` to reach nested blocks (or add an explicit finances path in the engine), give `ACTION_EFFECTS` entries a cost component, and check affordability against treasury/agent budget in the validation gate before applying.

title: Conflicting decisions do not interact: 'block_unfunded_spending' blocks nothing
evidence: engine.py:37 `"block_unfunded_spending": {"government_approval": -0.003}` — the action's only effect is an approval penalty on the actor's own side. The tick loop (engine.py:159-173) iterates institutions in list order and applies each proposal's deltas independently; there is no cross-proposal arbitration, no ordering semantics, no veto resolution. In kestral-strait.json the finance minister's objective is literally `"block_unfunded_defence_spending"` (:305) and the defence minister's is `"secure_new_patrol_vessels"` (:270); llm_gateway.py:44-45 makes both fire every tick. Both apply in full. The head-of-government's constraint `"coalition_partner_veto"` (:263) is likewise never evaluated.
why_it_matters: The demo scenario is built around an institutional conflict the engine cannot represent. CHARTER.md:21 "Officials have competing incentives" and the "Trigger an approval process → Procedural" primitive (CHARTER.md:99) have no implementation. Commitment 4 asks "which actors reacted" — with no interaction model, the answer is always "none reacted to each other".
remedy: Introduce a per-tick proposal arbitration stage: collect all proposals, resolve vetoes/blocks/approval prerequisites against each other and against agent authority, then apply survivors — logging both the applied and the blocked with the rule that blocked them.

title: Every legal/procedural constraint in kestral-strait.json is dead data — the engine never reads the field
evidence: The scenario defines constraints on five of six agents: kestral-strait.json:263 `["coalition_partner_veto", "emergency_powers_90day_renewal"]`; :291-295 `["procurement_law_18mo_minimum", "budget_ceiling_approval_required", "no_direct_foreign_arms_deal_without_cabinet_vote"]`; :317 `["parliamentary_budget_approval"]`; :342 `["treaty_non_binding_cooperation_only"]`; :364 `["no_independent_cyber_attribution", "reliant_on_western_sigint_sharing"]`. `MicroAgent.constraints` exists (agent_schema.py:181-183, "Hard legal/procedural constraints on the agent") and is parsed at engine.py:107 `spec = MicroAgent(**a)`. Grep for `constraint` across backend/app returns exactly two hits, both the schema field definition — zero reads. `win_conditions` (kestral-strait.json:419-436, incl. `max_emergency_powers_renewals: 1`) likewise: grep returns zero hits in backend/.
why_it_matters: The scenario file advertises a legal system that does not exist. The word "Hard" in the schema description is false — these are inert strings. CHARTER.md:16 "Governments have legal authority, but not unlimited authority" and CHARTER.md:114 "it never decides whether the composition is legal" are both unbacked, because nothing in the codebase decides legality at all. Win/loss conditions are also unevaluated, so a run has no terminal semantics.
remedy: Either implement a constraint evaluator that maps constraint tags to predicates over (proposal, agent, macro_state, tick), or mark `constraints` and `win_conditions` explicitly as not-yet-consumed scaffolding in the schema descriptions and README so they are not read as implemented behaviour.

title: The engine's own effects table violates the demo scenario's headline procurement law on tick 1
evidence: kestral-strait.json:292 constrains min-defence-oduya with `"procurement_law_18mo_minimum"`. llm_gateway.py:44 makes `minister_of_defence` propose `request_procurement_acceleration` every single tick. engine.py:36 prices it `{"military_readiness": 0.01, "government_approval": -0.002}` and engine.py:164 applies it immediately. `Intervention.timeline_days` (agent_schema.py:245-247, "Days until the action takes effect") is never read — grep returns only the schema definition. Starting `military_readiness` is 0.39 (kestral-strait.json:21); with +0.01/tick and the 0..1 clamp at macro_state.py:43-44, readiness ratchets monotonically to the ceiling by tick 61, with no cooldown, no budget check and no delay.
why_it_matters: This is the single sharpest contradiction in the repo: the scenario says procurement takes 18 months minimum, and the engine grants readiness instantly, every tick, forever. CHARTER.md:20 "Procurement takes time" and CHARTER.md:19 "Money has to come from somewhere" are both directly falsified by the demo path that runs by default. Note this is not merely 'unimplemented' — `test_macro_state_actually_changes` (test_engine.py:52-58) asserts on exactly this ratchet, so the defect is load-bearing for a passing test.
remedy: Gate `request_procurement_acceleration` on a scheduled-effect queue honouring `timeline_days`, and debit `resource_cost` from treasury/agent budget. At minimum, add a cooldown so a single action cannot be re-applied every tick.

title: The player-intervention endpoint accepts any action and applies nothing — and its module docstring claims otherwise
evidence: `POST /api/simulation/runs/{run_id}/decision` exists (routes_simulation.py:80-101) and accepts an arbitrary `Intervention`. Its body only appends a log dict with `"effects": []` (routes_simulation.py:91-100) and returns `{"accepted": True}` (:101) — unconditionally true, for any actor_role and any action_type, including ones absent from `ACTION_EFFECTS`. The module docstring at routes_simulation.py:4-5 states "Player decisions are parsed into `Intervention` objects — the engine validates and prices them". The function docstring at :82 says validation "happens on tick" — it does not: the tick loop (engine.py:147-180) reads only `inst.last_proposal` from institutional agents and never inspects `event_log` or any pending-intervention queue. The `Intervention` type is never imported by engine.py.
why_it_matters: So a player-intervention path exists in name only; there is no route by which a human decision can ever reach `_validate_and_price` or change a number. `accepted: True` for an unrecognised or illegal action is an affirmatively misleading API response. This also undercuts commitment 1's "same decisions" clause — decisions currently cannot affect numbers at all, so the reproducibility test is vacuous with respect to decisions.
remedy: Queue submitted interventions on the model and drain them through the same gate as agent proposals during `step()`, returning a typed accepted/rejected verdict instead of a hardcoded True. Meanwhile correct routes_simulation.py:4-5 — the function docstring's honesty (:83-85) should be promoted to the module header, not contradicted by it.
